data.title: January Ifo Index Pours Cold Water On Hopes Of A Stellar Rebound For The German Economy
data.description:
By Carsten Brzeski, Global Head of Macro

This morning, Germany has literally slid into weather and traffic chaos. While this chaos will only be temporary, the just-released Ifo index suggests that leaving the economic chaos behind will be more cumbersome than seeing today’s snow melt away.

In January, Germany’s leading indicator, the Ifo index, remained unchanged at 87.6. While the current assessment improved somewhat, expectations weakened. The reading should be taken with a pinch of salt, as it is unclear whether most participants sent in their answers before or after US President Donald Trump’s decision not to impose additional tariffs on several European countries.

More generally speaking, the unchanged Ifo index reflects the uncertainty that has hit the German economy again on the back of geopolitical tensions and tariff threats.

Time to get more positive about Germany

Despite this morning's disappointing Ifo index reading, there are good reasons to be more positive about the German economy. The latest macro data indicates a clear turning point in industry at the end of last year. Industrial orders have now increased for three consecutive months, and even the argument that bulk orders drove the November surge does not really concern us; with the fiscal spending programme, more of these bulk orders will come this year. In fact, bulk orders could be the new normal, not the exception.

While industry is going through a soft and tentative period of cyclical turning, admittedly at still shockingly weak levels, the announced infrastructure and defence investment plans should finally begin to reach the economy this year. Critics often overlook the sluggishness of Germany’s federal decision-making process. It took until late last year for parliament to approve the 2026 budget and almost 30 military procurement contracts.

With the rapid expansion of defence production capacity, there is a good chance that a large part of defence spending will remain within the domestic economy and not leak to other countries. We see a clearly higher multiplier for defence investments than anticipated a year ago. Last but not least, even though corporates never tire of complaining about elevated energy costs, the government’s decision to bring those costs down to one-third of their current levels should bring further relief, if implemented.

Biggest risk: from national depression to national complacency

Even if today's Ifo index undermines some of our optimism, an economic liftoff is clearly in the making. The Ifo index reading, however, does reflect the uncertainty that has hit the German economy again on the back of geopolitical tensions and tariff threats.

As much as we remain convinced of the upcoming economic recovery, we continue to stress that the economy’s problems are deeply rooted, often structural and largely self-made, except for the China problem. Solving these issues quickly is impossible. This is a completely different challenge from some 20 years ago, when Germany was the ‘sick man of Europe’. Ongoing geopolitical tensions and the everlasting threat and uncertainty regarding US tariffs are further complicating factors for Germany’s economic outlook.

Despite any cyclical upswing, the economy still needs an almost complete makeover, ranging from well-known measures such as reducing red tape and introducing e-government to mastering and lowering the financial burden of demographics or through tax cuts. It is up to German Chancellor Friedrich Merz and his government to implement these reforms this year and turn a long-awaited rebound into a sustainable recovery.

The too-long period of de facto stagnation seems to be coming to an end. However, the list of potential downside risks is still long. And while there is very little Germany can do to prevent new external shocks, it has full and absolute control over not shifting from one extreme to the other: the biggest domestic risk remains any sudden shift from national depression to national complacency.

Content Disclaimer

This publication has been prepared by ING solely for information purposes irrespective of a particular user's means, financial situation or investment objectives. The information does not constitute investment recommendation, and nor is it investment, legal or tax advice or an offer or solicitation to purchase or sell any financial instrument. Read more.

data.title: Nucor: 15% Upside Potential Driven By Investment Completion And Strategic Growth
data.description:
Executive Summary

Nucor (NUE) is a perfect choice for investors seeking a position in the US steel sector due to its operational excellence. We are positive on the stock both for the sector's growth outlook (+2.2% cagr in the period 2024-2030) and for company-specific reasons, such as the positive impact of the last few years investments on earnings from 2026.

Therefore, we have a buy rating with a $207 target price, based on our dcf model. It Implies a 15% upside potential from January 21, 2026, closing price of $ 180.23.

Company Description

Nucor is the largest and most diversified American steel and steel products manufacturer. The company's operations are heavily concentrated in the US, where it generates 98% of its revenues.

With regards to segment breakdown, the Steel Mills segment (production of sheet bars, structural steel, and plates) generates 62% of revenues, the Steel Products segment (joists, decking, fasteners, and tubing) accounted for 33% and the Raw materials segment the remaining 5%.

The company's strength is its use of electric arc furnace (EAF) technology, which offers much more flexible production management. Indeed, unlike traditional blast furnaces, which must operate continuously for years (because shutting them down would cause significant structural damage), EAFs can be shut down and restarted quickly based on market demand or energy costs. Furthermore, electric technology allows for much more precise and faster temperature control (up to 3000°C), facilitating the production of special steels and high-quality alloys.

Nucor has outperformed both the S&P 500 (total return of 47% versus 16%) and the S&P Materials sector index (8.4%) over the last 12 months. In our view, the outperformance is attributable to two elements:

1) The Trump administration's increase in US steel imports tariffs from 25% to 50% for most countries, except for Europe, which has a 15% tariff rate.

2) The release of better-than-expected quarterly results. In Q3 2025, the company posted revenues of $8.52 billion, exceeding consensus estimates of $8.22 billion. Ebitda reached $1.3 billion, and eps was $2.63, up 18.5% versus the consensus estimate of $2.22. The Steel Products segment led profit growth thanks to strong demand for rebar and data center products, while the Steel Mills segment saw a decline in the latter due to lower profit margins.

Investment Case

We think that Nucor could benefit over the next few quarters from several favorable elements:

1. Steel demand in the US should grow at a healthy pace in the coming years led by a recovery in the manufacturing and non-residential construction sectors for the expansionary fiscal and monetary policies. Investments in data centers and the electricity distribution network, necessary for AI development, could also have a significant impact on US steel demand. According to several research institutions, steel demand could grow at a 2.2% cagr between 2024 and 2030.

2. The last few years capex made to increase production and existing plants more efficient should begin to bear fruit during 2026. At the time of publishing the Q3 2025 results, the management announced that 80% of the $10 billion investment program had been completed. In this scenario, the next few years results should benefit from both the impact of these investments and the capex reduction.

3. The order backlog remains significant at 3.5 million tons (+30% year-on-year).

4. The company's valuation (see section below) remains attractive despite the recent stock price increase. Based on our Unlevered dcf model and multiple analysis, we estimate a fair value between $207, with a 15% upside potential from January 21, 2026, closing price of $180.23.

What Could Go Wrong

The main risk to our positive stance on Nucor is a US investment slowdown. The manufacturing and non-residential construction sectors could suffer from weak growth if the Fed's recent round of rate cuts prove insufficient to revive economic activity. Moreover, the data center sector could suffer should the enthusiasm for ai development wanes. Other factors that could negatively impact the results include an increase in electricity prices or potential delays in the full operation of the West Virginia plant and other facilities in which the company has invested, delaying the impact on cash flow.

Valuation

We value Nucor using both a dcf model and multiple analysis. Our dcf assumptions include:

  • Ebit growing at 7.4% cagr in the period 2024-2030 as the Company could benefit from the last few years’ capex to increase operating margins.
  • Capex peaking in 2025 and 2026 as the $10 billion capex plan is completed.
  • Wacc of 8.0%.
  • A terminal growth rate of 2.0%, in line with the projected potential real long-term growth rate of global economy.

This yields a of $207 target Price, implying a 15% upside potential from January 21, 2026, closing price.

A multiple analysis highlights a less favorable picture on the stock. The stock is trading at an estimated 2026 P/E of 15.4x above the 2016-2025 average of 13.7x. The P/E would return below the average in 2027 (13.1x). However, at our target price calculated with the dcf model, the stock would trade at a 2026 P/E of 17.7x, well above the long-term average but below the 2025 P/E (21.9x). However, we believe that better earning perspectives for the coming years and the recent Company’s focus on higher value-added products, as indicated by the growing share of the Steel Products segment on total revenue (from 24% in 2021 to 33% in 9M ’25) deserve higher multiple valuation.

The View from the Street

Analysts covering the stock at major investment firms have a positive view on Nucor: twelve analysts have a buy rating, three a hols rating, and none have a sell rating.

Despite the positive opinion on the stock, the consensus target price of $182.38 is in line with current prices, well below our target price ($207). However, we believe it could be revised upward in the coming months, in line with what happened in the 2021-2024 period.

Conclusion

Despite having already shown a solid upward trend over the last 12 months for the better-than-expected results and the Trump administration's increased tariffs on steel imports, we believe Nucor still has an interesting upside potential. The stock should benefit from strong US steel demand, confirmed by the order backlog, and the positive impact of the completion of the $10 billion investment plan implemented in recent years. We assign a buy rating on Nucor stock with a $207 target price.

data.title: Siemens: A Case To Be Made For The Macro-Related Downside
data.description:
Going contrarian against a solid, €200B market cap company is not exactly an easy thing. This is especially true when the company in question is one of very few global AA-rated conglomerates. It's further very difficult when that company is superbly managed and owns significant or majority stakes in companies that you (or I, in this case) love owning and also own stakes in.

But there comes a point when despite a company's theoretical upside in the case of further growth, there's a downside risk so significant and out of the company's control that investing in the business is simply not a good idea.

Siemens (SIEGY) is currently in that position - and I don't foresee that changing.

My last coverage on Siemens was about 2-3 months ago, but I have been covering the stock for as long as I have been writing on Seeking Alpha. In my early career, I owned over 8% of my total portfolio in the company - and part of me is obviously sad, given the returns since, that I did not keep that. At the same time, my overall portfolio performance has been good or fair even compared to the solid return that Siemens has generated.

In this article, I will be offering assumptions and forecasting for Siemens for 2026-2028E, and why I believe the forecasted double-digit EPS growth is unlikely to materialize, or at the very least, too risky to base an investment thesis on. I will argue that there are better alternatives, even within the Siemens 'sphere of influence' and that you could at the very least consider these.

What I am arguing here is essentially the following: It's not that Siemens is a bad business or unlikely to grow - it's that further growth at the pace that's being forecasted, which is justifying this company's significant premium, is unlikely given the secular weaknesses in certain end markets as well as certain mixes of macro trends.

Now let me show you what I mean with this.

Siemens - Upside here is unlikely given results, but more importantly, volatile end markets and macro trends

I won't spend much time on going through the qualities of Siemens here, the positive ones. These are all well-established, and I focus on them in previous articles. What I instead will show you is why the company is unlikely to see further significant growth.

Siemens, as it remains today, is a mix of attractive business areas, but one of those business areas is the Digital Industries, or DI segment. This focuses on things like automation and digitization of processes, specifically for companies in manufacturing. Siemens is a full-service provider, offering both hardware solutions in EDA as well as software solutions for this, covering the spectrum from industrial automation to digital transformation. Major end-users and markets for this include automotive, aerospace and various machining and construction businesses.

Recent results in the company's reports have focused in part on a slowdown of growth in the DI segment. As tends to be the case with explosive growth segments or areas like this, there comes a time when high growth turns to low growth. Following the post-Covid-19 period, there were long years of very high demand for these services. But trends not only out of Siemens but peers as well suggest that customers are destocking existing inventories in automation and digitalization in a variety of areas. This impacts the short-cycle results and trends in the business. And while this for now has been disguised a bit by upside in Siemens Energy (SMEGF) and other things, the signs are there for anyone wanting to look at them, including the company cutting jobs.

These signs include weak demand from both Germany and China, which for Siemens are key geographies, as well as an increased competition and peer presence. While Siemens is a market-leader here, it's not immune to the margin pressure that arises when new players enter the market, and that's what's happening here. Furthermore, competition for this is not just coming from smaller, more agile players, but Siemens-like peers like ABB (ABB), Schneider (SBGSY), and Rockwell (ROK). The coming of AI has also enabled startups focused entirely on this. It's not incorrect, therefore, to call the space more crowded than before. The barriers to entry have been levelled thanks to no more requirements for coding proficiency, with tools that enable both low- and no-code solutions.

While this is no end to Siemens DI, it impacts the segment and the business, and that is my argument. The company still has a large backlog here, but I argue it's disguising trends that over the coming 2-5 years will not be as favorable as analysts are claiming that they will.

Aside from competition in DI, we can look at the rise of increased competition in most of the areas where Siemens was/is expecting significant and above-average growth. The issue is that even if Mobility and Electrification are posting good results, these are lower-margin segments compared to automation - and if automation and DI margins collapse or go lower, then this would impact Siemens as a whole. And the expectations for both Mobility and Electrification are down as well - not just from me, but from other analysts and the market, such as Erste Group, which in 2025 clarified its position.

The thing is, even if the segments performed as expected, or at relatively high levels, you'd be investing in Siemens at a valuation that by all accounts is extremely stretched. I will go into it more in the valuation section, but the fact is that has only traded at this level at one time the past 20 years - right before the GFC. This is not a favorable outlook by any historical or forward-looking measure.

I'm not forecasting another GFC, or GFC-like crash, where Siemens went from a peak of €95/share, which then was 25-26x P/E, to less than €38/share about 1-2 years later, but I am saying that analysts would be very wise to consider the current levels of valuation, what they imply, and the current levels of return and what those imply.

The implications for these, when put in context with things like the DI performance, the underlying automation, electrification, mobility, AI and other markets (and trends in those markets), do not "gel" all that well with an even higher share price. Frankly, even if the company fulfilled all of its targets and expectations during the next 2-3 years, I would view the company as even more inflated than before.

Siemens - Upside for the company based on valuation and expectations

One thing you have to understand about me and how I invest is the following: It's far more important to me to avoid losses compared to securing market-beating gains.

That means that I will go out of my way to avoid investments that I view as higher risk or that might lower my overall capital, even if those investments, according to some, have a high likelihood of success.

This sort of strategy is suited for the risk-averse, the older investor, the person who's "made their nut", and doesn't want to lose it, even at the cost of somewhat lower returns or "missing out" on some things.

So know this going in - if you're the type of more risk-tolerant and even risk-seeking investor, then you'll need to consider my targets and assumptions with this in mind. If I was more risk-taking and accepting of higher targets, my PT for Siemens would look different. A bullish investor might look at Siemens and give the company a 25-26x P/E on a forward basis, despite the fact that the company has never averaged this in 20+ years, or that even at 26x P/E, your RoR would be only barely about 16.5% per year.

It would also ignore that the 20-year average is closer to 17x P/E, and the highest average is about 20x, with longer-term averages implying a RoR close to negative (or below 2% per year, inclusive of the yield, meaning negative without the company's dividend). That means that a savings account is giving you better RoR than Siemens forecasted at a conservative valuation with these assumptions.

That is not a particularly favorable situation to "enter" into, no matter how solid the fundamentals or the prospects might be, in your estimate.

Consider also, for a moment, the fact that Siemens AG retains a 35%+ stake in Siemens Energy, which has been on an absolute tear for about 1 year. That means that at least part of the reason that Siemens is up the performance of Siemens Energy. Conversely, if you believe that SMEGF has gone up too far, too quickly, you should consider the impacts this might have on Siemens as well. This is my stance.

Siemens is more and more a pure play on automation. This is further confirmed by the company's recent M&As, which all to some extent focus on automation and DI. It does not make the other businesses bad, but it underscores the problems that could arise if a secular underperformance in DI was to come.

I forecast no more than a 4-5% longer-term AEPS growth because I believe DI and smart infrastructure will grow lower than expected. I don't expect a cyclical recovery in DI to post-COVID-19 trends, but I believe this spike to have been a one-time thing. The order backlog (extensive) does not change my thesis. I assume that the electrification trend that's ongoing will be less than expected globally, and with a longer-term industrial profit margin of 15-16% at most, I come to a 17x forward P/E, the 15-20 year average. Most analysts who forecast the company higher, at say €250-€300/share, assume 17-19% margins and a significant tailwind from DI recovery.

To me, this implies a €250/share fair value, not a PT - the PT, risks included, is closer to €175/share - this was my share price target in my last article, and I'm doubling down on it here.

Risks to the company are as follows.

Risks & Considerations

Risks for Siemens include, aside from potential margin pressures and the risks mentioned in this article not only to DI and to Siemens Energy as a part of the thesis, a dilution from its diversified portfolio relative to AI.

It remains unfavorable when you compare it to straight-electrical equipment manufacturers among its peers, like Schneider or Legrand, both of which I cover (and one of which I invest in).

As mentioned above, Siemens' strategy at this point of its growth trajectory is mostly based on inorganic growth, which obviously comes with the risk of overpaying for assets (which I believe the company is already doing, such as when it bought Altair for over $10B at over 30x P/E, which is almost twice Siemens own valuation over time).

Aside from that, its portfolio structure needs to be considered. The issues in Energy were clearly ones that showed us how they can affect the holding company's business and valuation.

Thesis

Siemens is a beyond-solid company that I've covered for years and years. Valuation, or the premiumization of this, however, means that I only maintain a small coverage position of below 0.1% as of this update in 2026. I believe valuation shows us, together with forecasts, why we should not be buying at this valuation, and I believe the risks related to DI and automation are very significant here.

At the right price, Siemens Aktiengesellschaft becomes a "BUY" strong enough to make me ignore or put second most other investments, and I would then put 1-3% of my total capital to work to get exposure here. But this would require a 30-40% drop from the levels we see in January.

My PT (price target) for Siemens remains at €175, updated for the 2026E period, even with the positive estimates for growth. I consider these estimates to be too exuberant to be trusted as a basis for investing, and therefore rate Siemens a "HOLD".

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (italicized).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this and a price target of €175/share, I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of January 2026.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Apple: Q1 Results Could Trigger A Price Reversal, But Buy Regardless
data.description:
Apple (AAPL) is down by over 10 percent from when my last bullish article on the name was published. In that piece, I expanded on an earlier thesis about the generational demand pull for iPhones in general, but made more specific by iPhone 17 demand being a pull-forward force for FY 2026.

Now that we are approaching Q1 2026 earnings after the market closes this Thursday, the 29th, it seems to be a good time to let investors know whether the decline from the recent peak in early December 2025 was justified, or if there is an opportunity here to take advantage of the market's inevitable reaction next week.

I do expect some price action at earnings, but will it be to the upside or will we see a continuance of the current decline to even farther below $250? That is what I hope to answer today, to the best of my ability.

What Triggered the Decline?

Our first angle of discovery should be the stock price decline itself, to see whether it was a reflection of larger market dynamics or just specific to AAPL.

There does not seem to be any evidence of a broad market impact on AAPL stock. On December 2, the stock was far ahead of not just the S&P 500 (SP500) but also the top two sectors of the past year - tech and communications.

Clearly, this is not a sectoral or broad-market phenomenon. I knew it was not, but I had to be sure to rule it out.

So, what did trigger the early December decline?

In reality, the indicators of a potential decline came in two weeks before that, when the Financial Times reported that the Cupertino smartphone behemoth was "stepping up its succession planning efforts, as it prepares for Tim Cook to step down as chief executive as soon as next year." Shortly after this report, the stock did drop, but quickly recovered to scale to the December 2 high.

While this somewhat weakened the sentiment around the stock, the real shocker came in early December - the management "shakeup" reported that week. Among those exiting was the senior VP of ML and AI Strategy, John Giannandrea.

In and of itself, that would hardly have been the reason to trigger a sell-off, but the sudden departure of several senior members of management following on from the news of Cook's reported exit in 2026 was a little too much for the market to handle in my view.

There were - and are - several other developments that contributed to the momentum and the longevity of the decline, but the implications of these news items is likely to have caused at least a small minority of investors to sell off. That makes sense to me because AAPL was trading near all-time highs. This is when the less serious bulls tend to take profits off the table or resize their positions to maintain the weighting.

The broader picture here is Apple finally admitting implicitly that its AI ambitions are moving in a different direction. From directly focusing on AI investments, it is pivoting to third-party solutions.

This began long before the January 12 news of a Gemini-infused Siri broke. Just before the middle of November, Bloomberg carried a report about Apple's lack of aggressive spending on AI CapEx. More recently, it was reported that Craig Federighi initiated a shift in AI strategy as early as last fall.

Federighi apparently addressed a joint meeting of Apple's software and AI teams, expressing enthusiasm for closer collaboration while also signaling dissatisfaction with the company's pace of progress in artificial intelligence. Some members of Apple's foundation models team interpreted the remarks as criticism of their work.

In December, Apple moved to consolidate its AI leadership under Federighi, completing a transition that had begun earlier in the year when responsibility for ‌Siri‌ was removed from the AI group and brought under Federighi's software division. In January, Apple announced plans to use Google's Gemini AI models to power future AI upgrades, including an improved version of ‌Siri‌. In Federighi's view, integrating a third-party model would allow Apple to finally ship a revamped ‌Siri‌ later this year after controversially postponing the update in 2025.

It appears that the confluence of these developments was gaining momentum well before the decline began in December, when the management shakeup occurred.

One can actually see this investor sentiment play out from as early as August 2025, when put call ratios started climbing. The ratio of put-call volumes (black line) speaks for itself, peaking at 3.4 in early December. Now why does that time-window sound familiar, I wonder?

Even more telling is the movement of OTM put call ratios, which generally indicate hedging by institutional investors at levels that are significantly above 1. In the case of AAPL, that ratio stood above 2.2 when the stock peaked.

I think that is sufficient evidence that the market was worried about Apple's AI pivot, but I also believe that the market has now digested the information fully, and could be on the verge of price reversal and a fresh rally on the back of Q1 earnings this week. Current put call levels are much more subdued. While ATM puts are still significant higher than calls, the OTM ratio is now comfortably below 1.0.

Why This Should Matter To You

Most analysts would understandably use the current decline to peddle a 'buy the dip' thesis, but that thesis can prove dangerous when one is unaware of what caused the decline in the first place. So, rather than give you a generic pill like 'unjustified sell-off' and simply hit you over the head with Apple's strong fundamentals to validate that stance, I propose to give you a meaningful reason why the decline is not a sign of any kind of deterioration in AAPL's future prospects.

That being said, more than fundamentals, market perception seems to be guiding AAPL's price right now. We have seen that clearly in the analysis above with the put call ratios rising, and while fundamentals provide a sort of valuation floor, it is market sentiment that gives it seasonal volatility around this time of the year, when the market is trying to guess which way the fundamentals will move based on the latest iPhone's success - or lack thereof, as the case may be.

Therefore, it stands to reason that market sentiment is what will guide the price when Q1 2026 earnings are out next week. The market will look at the headline figures, fingers hovering nervously over the virtual buttons to buy, sell or hedge. Once the perception has been formed, the sentiment takes over.

That means we need to begin with a preview of what might come.

What Market Will Be Looking At for Q1

There were several indicators that iPhone 17 demand was very strong, the most important of which was the CEO's projections reported at the end of October, a month into the crucial holiday quarter.

In an interview with Reuters, Cook said he expects iPhone sales in the current, holiday-focused quarter to grow by double digits year over year and Apple's overall revenue to grow 10-12% year over year. Those forecasts for Apple's fiscal first quarter of 2026 beat analyst estimates of iPhone sales rising 9.8% to $75.91 billion and total sales up 6.6% to $132.53 billion, according to data from LSEG.

If those projections are accurate, that alone is sufficient reason to expect the stock to pop at earnings because it will create a surge of positive sentiment.

But why do I think that will happen?

Apple has generally been conservative with guidance, as evidenced by its pristine EPS beat record over the past two years.

In case you missed it, Cook's guidance was for revenue growth, so why am I using earnings to make the point?

My view is that earnings growth is the real focus for Apple, especially at Q1.

The top line is important from a headline perspective, of course, because it reflects the strength or weakness of the iPhone model released for that fiscal year. Earnings, on the other hand, are material to the price of the stock and are more within the company's direct control. SG&A and R&D outlays are carefully expensed to maximize operating profitability, which flows directly into net margins.

Going a step further, I would also add "maximize cash flows" to that statement because it is the consistently strong cash flow generation that keep the share buyback engine running smoothly quarter after quarter, and this is a crucial component of what supports that valuation floor I mentioned above.

What I Shall be Looking At for Q1 2026

Now that we know that it is earnings and cash flows that power investor sentiment around AAPL stock, what can we expect?

The Street's expectations are implicit for the next several years, and optimism is baked into the stock on the sell side. For this quarter, in particular, these analysts are looking at a growth rate of about 11.5 percent at the bottom line, off a revenue growth rate of a similar 11.4 percent.

These optimistic expectations are not out of place considering Cook's guidance from October, but this actually adds on more risk than ever because a miss at these levels would only serve to trigger a further downward momentum in the stock.

Assuming Apple beats on both lines (but more importantly on adjusted EPS), there is still no guarantee of a reversal in sentiment. Remember that AAPL declined in the weeks after its Q4/FY 2025 results came out at the end of October.

I shall attribute this decline to the fact that the Q4 double-beat was marginal at the top (0.21 percent to the upside), and not very strong at the bottom (4.45 percent to the upside).

Far more relevant to my thesis as it stands today ahead of Q1 earnings is the fact that there has been a significant deterioration of market sentiment around AAPL stock.

This is where it gets challenging to defend a Buy recommendation ahead of the Q1 release, but I posit that the stock is very likely to see that reversal at earnings.

My first clue is Apple's forward earnings multiple, which stands right at its three-year average.

One could certainly argue that it is completely logical for any variable to oscillate around its historical average because that is precisely how the average is calculated, but looking beyond the mathematical phenomenon, one sees that the average also appears to be a line of support/resistance for the earnings multiple, depending on whether it is expanding or compressing.

One should also observe that these valuation moves have very little correlation with the actual earnings results. This is better seen on a price chart.

I find it extremely intriguing that, notwithstanding the rare exception, no earnings release has ever triggered a reversal of the stock's price decline. This is despite a string of adjusted EPS beats over the period in question. In other words, none of the bottoms have ever been marked by an earnings announcement.

What I Shall Take Away Ahead of Q1 Results

My takeaway from this analysis is that the stock may continue to decline despite a single or double beat at Q1. Negative sentiment appears to currently overwhelm any market bullishness on AAPL.

On the other hand, if that sentiment is captured as a moment-in-time snapshot, it would appear that the market is leaning slightly toward bullish. January 30 put and call volatility does not appear overly skewed.

So, on the balance, a decline is certainly not off the table, but if the beat at the bottom line is strong enough, it could provide enough momentum for a reversal. At the very least, it will reassure investors that Apple can still keep growing its earnings and cash flows despite the new direction it has chosen to take on the AI front - that of a 'reseller' of others' AI products.

Despite the continued decline into 2026, the market has still not made up its mind about AAPL. That is why its stock price in the week leading to earnings resembles an expectant father furiously pacing up and down the corridors of the maternity ward. He is, after all, not an 'insider'.

This is how I see the short term dynamics: an apprehensive market waiting on a strongly optimistic iPhone 17 performance to offset worries of a further decline. If that decline does continue after Q1 earnings are out, it would indicate that the negative sentiment has yet to play out, and it might be a while before the stock finds its legs again.

From a long-term perspective, however, the negative sentiment around Apple dropping out of the AI race against the other mega-caps is more than balanced out by the possibilities of leveraging its massive and still-growing hardware install base as a 'delivery mechanism' for the best AI utilities in the market.

My Biggest Takeaway

That implies strong services growth over the next several years, and that will continue to bring more stability into the company's fiscal performance year after year.

In summation, while I do see some downside potential in the short term, I would still advise a staggered Buy before and after earnings. An equal split is good enough because the decision is binary but will still benefit you - if the stock continues to decline, you bought half the tranche at a more expensive cost basis but benefit from the lower price post earnings; on the flip side, if stock price sees a clear reversal, you still bought half the tranche at a lower cost basis, and now there is more upside to look forward to.

It is a clear win-win as I see it, but please remember that the prerequisite to following this recommendation is your personal sentiment on the stock and your long-term view of the company's growth trajectory. I have laid out my long-term bullish outlook over the course of these last three articles, including this one, because I remain convinced of the long-term attractiveness of this stock regardless of short-term price action. Can you say the same? That is the real question here, is it not?

data.title: Rithm Capital: Get Paid Rich Dividends While Waiting For A Valuation Upgrade
data.description:
I previously covered Rithm Capital Corp. (NYSE:RITM) in July 2025, discussing how the mREIT was finally trading near its book value, thanks to the robust performance metrics and the normalizing yield curve in the short-and-long term interest rates, with it naturally reducing the risks of a potential recession.

Thanks to its rich dividend yields, I had rated the stock as a Buy upon a moderate retracement then, despite the potentially prolonged re-rating nearer to its mature alternative asset management peers.

In this article, I shall discuss why I am reiterating my Buy rating for the RITM stock here, thanks to the well covered/secure dividend incomes, the management's laser focus on delivering diversified mREIT/asset management growth opportunities, the speculative upward re-rating in its valuations, and the consequently rich capital appreciation prospects.

This is despite the likely to be lumpy FQ4'25 performance arising from the ongoing Fed rate cut/recent acquisitions and the stock's sideways trading cadence over the past few years.

RITM's Diversification Continues

Since my last Buy rating, RITM's correction has indeed occurred to the $10.60s before rallying to the $11.70s by the time of writing, with a similar recovery also observed in its mREIT peers in varying degrees.

Part of the pullback in September 2025 may be attributed to the issuance of preferred stocks, including Series E and Series F, along with the $750M of capital raise, as the management aggressively raise cash to fund its recent acquisition spree, including:

  • Paramount Group (NYSE:PGRE), an office REIT for ~$1.6B,
  • $1B in home improvement loans from Upgrade, Inc, a fintech company that offers affordable and responsible credit and mobile banking to mainstream consumers, and
  • Crestline Management, an alternative investment manager with approximately $17 billion in assets under management.

This development lends credence to RITM's ongoing diversification efforts away from the existing mortgage origination/servicing business as they attempt to grow their portfolio in the alternative asset management rather aggressively, albeit by diluting shareholders and raising credit through third party capital/partners.

Upon the closing of these acquisitions, the mREIT expects to report $109B in pro forma investable assets, up moderately from the last reported sum of $80B in FQ2'25 earnings call, with the management expecting to draw on financing facilities of $1.14B to arrive at a proforma cash position of $1.27B.

It is unsurprising that RITM has embarked on this path, since the market is entering a lower cost borrowing environment in 2026, with it presenting headwinds to its intermediate term prospects.

While the value of mortgage servicing rights has previously risen as interest rates have risen over the past few years, it is apparent that things may no longer be the same, with MSRs likely to decline in value as the Feds continue their rate cuts.

This devaluation trend may also be why RITM has doubled down on their diversification efforts while attempting to drive higher valuation through their "differentiated model and operating platforms."

RITM's Discounted Valuations Unwarranted

For reference, RITM remains inherently discounted as an mREIT at TTM Price/Book valuations of 0.93x and FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations of 5.48x, compared to the sector median at 1.34x and 11.52x, respectively.

The same conclusion may also be derived when comparing to its mREIT peers, including:

  • Annaly Capital Management (NLY) at 1.24x/8.20x,
  • AGNC Investment Corp. (AGNC) at 1.33x/7.70x,
  • Dynex Capital, Inc. (DX) at 1.14x/15.37x,
  • Two Harbors Investment Corp. (TWO) at 1.19x/10.20x, and
  • PennyMac Mortgage Investment Trust (PMT) at 0.86x/11.19x, respectively,

with it apparent that RITM offers a value-oriented mREIT investment thesis - as similarly observed in its notably lower, single digits forward dividend yields of 8.39% (compared to its peers' double digits yields).

While some may argue that RITM's discounted valuations may be attributed to its frozen dividend payouts at an annualized sum of $1 since late 2021 and the notably low EAD payout ratio at 46.2% in FQ3'25 (inline YoY), I beg to differ, since a similar trend has been observed in some of its mREIT peers in varying degrees, including:

  • NLY with a recent payout hike by +7.7%, EAD coverage ratio of 95.8%, and forward dividend yields of 11.74%,
  • AGNC with stagnant payouts since early 2020, 46.1%, and 12.21%,
  • DX with 3Y dividend growth rate at +9%, 42.5%, and 14.13%,
  • TWO with 3Y dividend decline at -16.81%, -44.1%, and 10.14%, and
  • PMT with 3Y dividend decline at -4.03%, 72.7%, and 12.08%, respectively,

with it apparent that RITM's discounted valuations may be unwarranted, especially since its ongoing diversification may trigger the alternative assets comprising a larger share of its overall pro forma investable assets at an estimated sum of ~56.8% in FQ4'25 (+11.8 points QoQ).

Metrics To Monitor Entering FQ4'25 Earnings Call

As a result of their promising diversification progress, I urge readers to closely monitor RITM's upcoming FQ4'25 earnings call on February 3, 2026, since it remains to be seen if the asset management revenues may eventually comprise a larger sum of its overall revenues, currently at a measly sum of 7.6% in FQ3'25 (-0.5 points YoY).

On the one hand, I urge readers to temper their expectations, since I expect to see moderate volatility in its MSR hedging results and net interest income (based on interest income minus interest expense/warehouse line fees) attributed to the three Fed rate cut in late 2025, with it likely to present near-term noise in its top/bottom-line performance.

At the same time, given the intensified diversification process through numerous acquisitions and the potentially higher integration costs in the near-term, I am of the opinion that RITM's dividend payouts are likely to remain flat ahead, with it likely to compress its valuations for a little longer.

This is especially since the management is funding its recent acquisitions through a mix of balance sheet and third party capital, with shareholder return likely taking a back seat as they aim to "deploy capital at, call it, 15% to 20% returns," as observed in the excellent return on equity of 18% in FQ3'25 (in line QoQ/YoY).

On the other hand, I am of the opinion that the lower borrowing cost environment may trigger RITM's expanded new mortgage origination/mortgage refinancing numbers on a QoQ/YoY basis, as similarly reported by Mortgage Bankers Association.

As a result of numerous potential outcomes entering FQ4'25 earnings call, I urge readers to tune in on February 3, 2026 indeed.

RITM Remains A Great Income Buy, Pending Upgrading

For now, RITM has mostly traded sideways at the $11s since 2024, offering interested investors great insight into its future stock price movement, pending an upward re-rating in its valuations.

I am of the opinion that at a time of Fed rate cut, the mREIT's forward dividend yields of 8.39% already offer interested and patient investors a rich quarterly income investment thesis.

Upon the speculative upward re-rating in its FWD P/E non-GAAP valuations nearer to the sector median of 11.52x, readers may look forward to an outsized capital appreciation prospect to my bull-case long-term price target of $27.10 as well, based on the consensus FY2027 EAD estimates of $2.36.

As a result of its well-balanced and secure dividend payouts in the near term along with the potentially rich, more than doubling upside potential over the next few years, thanks to the aggressive diversification efforts, I am reiterating my Buy rating for the RITM stock here.

data.title: Hints Of U.S. Treasury Intervention To Support The Yen
data.description:
The Man Who Helped Break The Bank Of England May Save The Bank Of Japan

Before becoming U.S. Treasury Secretary, Scott Bessent built his reputation in the hedge fund world. Early in his career, he rose to prominence at Soros Management, where he contributed to the firm’s legendary bet against the British Pound

Using Bessent’s research that the pound was overvalued, Soros Management made a $10 billion wager that the Bank of England would not continue to support their currency. It all came to a head on Wednesday, September 16, 1992, when the Bank of England abandoned using reserves to buy pounds, causing the currency to crash. Bessent’s firm made a tidy $1 billion profit.

This period is famously referred to as “Black Wednesday,” when Soros Management “Broke the Bank of England.”

I highlight Bessent’s risk taking nature in my recent Seeking Alpha article “Secretary Bessent Is Running The U.S. Treasury Like A Hedge Fund.”

Well, Bessent is back in the currency markets again. Only this time, as U.S Treasury Secretary, he is aiding the Bank of Japan ("BOJ") in supporting their currency.

The BOJ has a huge problem. They have been very slow in normalizing their monetary policy after an extended period of extreme easing. They are dealing with a delicate balance between new fiscal policy initiatives to prop up the economy while inflation remains persistently high. This has resulted in rising bond yields and a weak yen.

Possible Yen Intervention

There has been a flurry of activity in Japanese markets this past week. Prime Minister, Sanae Takaichi, who has only been in office for three months, proposed a new fiscal measure to temporarily eliminate the 8% consumption tax on food for two years. While this measure is appealing to voters who are dealing with the high cost of groceries, it creates a huge hole in the budget, with no clear solution as to how the tax cut would be funded.

Market concern that the revenue loss would be filled by new government borrowing caused long bond yields to spike, as investors worried that new debt would only increase Japan’s bloated borrowing. At a total debt to GDP level of over 250%, Japan is already the most indebted country in the world.

The market uncertainty was exacerbated by Takaichi calling for a snap election to be held on February 8th, only 18 days away. By dissolving the Lower House, whose term was to run until October 2028, the Prime Minister is hoping to capitalize on her popularity by strengthening her party and its coalition’s position in the parliament.

The foreign exchange value of the yen weakened leading up to these events.

On Friday, the BOJ announced that in an 8-1 vote, they decided to hold the policy rate at the current 0.75%. The lone dissenter voted to raise the policy rate an additional 25 basis points to 1.00% due to his concern about the elevated level of inflation.

At the post-meeting press briefing, BOJ Governor Kazuo Ueda was noncommittal about when the next rate hike might occur. With regard to bonds though, Ueda stated “Long-term interest rates are rising at quite a fast pace” and that “we are ready to take nimble action to cope with exceptional, unusual moves.”

As seen in the intra-day chart below, initially, the yen weakened to 159.25, on concern the BOJ wasn’t tightening enough to combat inflation and the new fiscal stimulus.

This was the yen’s highest level since June 2024 when the BOJ last intervened to support the currency at 160.00. It then rallied unexpectedly in anticipation that another intervention might occur.

The rally was strengthened several hours later when in New York, buzz began spreading that the New York Fed, at the behest of the Scott Bessent’s Treasury, was doing a “Rate Check” on what levels they could purchase Japanese Yen.

A rate check is considered a high-level warning that the government is concerned about the exchange rate and that a currency intervention might occur.

Sometimes, though, a rate check can be used as a way to give notice of an undesirable move in the currency, and cause positioning to create a reversal, without actually intervening.

Although the Fed is authorized to intervene in the foreign exchange (FX) markets, it is a rare event. There have only been three FX interventions in the past 30 years. The most recent was in 2011. Usually, the intervention is conducted in coordination with the central bank of the target currency.

At the end of the day, between the speculation in Japan about currency intervention, and the buzz in New York about the Treasury’s rate check, the yen rallied 1.75% to 155.63, This was the biggest 1-day surge since August.

Bigger Picture

Japan, like the rest of the G-7 economies, uses a target inflation rate of 2.0%.

CPI ex fresh foods and energy, otherwise known as Core Core CPI, is the BOJ’s preferred measure of inflation. As seen in the chart below, it broke above the desired 2.0% rate in October 2022. It has been above target ever since. Core Core for December 2025 came in at 2.9%.

While the Federal Reserve began Quantitative Tightening in March 2022l, the BOJ has been behind the curve. It took the BOJ an additional two years to follow suit. BOJ was the last man standing with their Negative Interest Rate Policy, which finally ended in March 2024.

Since then, the two central banks have been moving in opposite directions. The BOJ has raised their policy rate 4 times by a total of 85 basis points to the current level of 0.75%. Meanwhile, over the past 16 months the Fed has cut rates 6 times by a total of 175 basis points, to the current level of 3.75%. The yield spread differential between the BOJ’s and the Fed’s policy rates, at 300 basis points, is the narrowest it has been since August 2022.

On the longer end, Japanese Government Bond (JGB) yields have risen more significantly. The following chart shows the JGB yield curve when the BOJ ended their Negative Interest Rate Policy and today. While 3-month securities have risen by 79 basis points, 10-year JGB yields have risen 153 basis points and 30-year JGB yields are up 181 basis points.

The 10 yr JGB hit 2.38% this week, a 27-year high, and the 30 yr JGB set a new record at 3.92%. Additionally, 40-year JGB yields rose above 4.00% for the first time since they were introduced in 2007. Long yields surged as much as 35 basis points on Tuesday when the market was spooked by PM Takaichi’s stimulus package of spending increases and the consumption tax cuts, as well as her call for the snap election on February 8th.

Treasury Secretary Bessent Ties Rise In Treasury Yields To JGB Selloff

Speaking from Davos, Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent said that he believed the recent weakness in Treasury bonds is tied to the selloff in JGBs.

He downplayed that the threats from Denmark of selling their Treasury securities in protest of the US position on Greenland were of any significance. While Bessent was curt with his views on Denmark, he was not wrong. Denmark only holds $10 billion in Treasury securities, a mere 0.1% of the $9 trillion of Treasury securities held by foreigners.

What went unsaid, however, was the risk of Japan selling their Treasury holdings in order to support their currency. Compared with Denmark, Japan is a very significant owner of Treasury bonds. Their holdings of $1.2 trillion in Treasuries make then the largest foreign owner of Treasury securities.

Perhaps Bessent is helping out the BOJ through Treasury’s rate check, so they don’t have to sell their Treasuries.

We know Bessent is worried about the JGB influence on Treasuries, because a key component of his strategy at Treasury for overweighting Treasury bill issuance, is to have longer rates come down, in parallel, with short rates. That way Treasury can term out their debt issuance into longer maturities at lower rates.

So far that hasn’t happened.

Since the Fed began lowering the Fed Funds Rate in September 2024, there have been six rate cuts totaling 175 basis points in rate reduction, to 3.75%. During that same period, the yield on the 10-year Treasury note (US10Y) has risen 63 basis points to 4.26%.

While at the beginning of the period we had an inverted 10-year Treasury to Fed Funds spread, by -187 basis points, it has now flipped to be positively sloped by 51 basis points.

Deteriorating Liquidity In The JGB Market

One of the problems in the JGB market is that no one wants to own them.

The largest holder of JGBs is the BOJ, and they are paring their positions through their version of Quantitative Tightening. In 2010, the BOJ owned less than 10% of the JGB market, but over the ensuing 13-year period, when they were executing their aggressive Quantitative Easing policy, their JGB holdings grew dramatically. A key element of their aggressive easing was their Yield Curve Control Policy, where they continuously purchased JGB’s to defend the band around their 0.0% target yield for 10-year JGBs. These continuous purchases swelled the BOJ’s JGB position to 54% of total JGBs outstanding.

Since their QT program began, though, the BOJ’s JGB holdings have declined to a 50% share of the total outstanding. No one has stepped up to fill the void, hence the sharp rise in yields.

The deterioration in liquidity is best exemplified by the Bloomberg JGB Liquidity Index. The index measures the average yield difference to fair value. When the difference is small, the market is considered quite liquid. As the average yield widens from fair value, the index signals distressed conditions and a deterioration in liquidity.

During normal times, the index hovers around 1 basis point. Since the BOJ embarked on their QT program, and stopped buying JGBs, the index has deteriorated significantly. The bond market selloff described earlier, caused the index to spike to a record high of 10 basis points, before settling back to a still illiquid level of 8.7 basis points.

This is a tremendous source of concern now, as the Prime Minister’s new policies will cause debt issuance to increase into this illiquid market.

Conclusion

Treasury Secretary Bessent, who began his career making large currency bets by helping Soros Management “Break the Bank of England,” is returning to his hedge fund ways.

On Friday, at the behest of Secretary Bessent’s U.S. Treasury, the New York Fed conducted a rate check among currency dealers to determine where they might be able to buy yen. The rate check is a loud signal that they are not happy with the current exchange rates, and an indication that currency intervention might occur.

The rate check caused the yen to rally 1.75%, it’s largest one day move in five months.

Last week, while attending the World Economic Forum in Davos, Secretary Bessent indicated that he thought the weakness in the U.S. Treasury market had been impacted by the selloff in JGBs. He also stated that “I’ve been in touch with my economic counterpart in Japan, and I am sure that they will begin saying the things that will calm the market down.”

While President Trump has been railing about the Fed cutting the Fed Funds rate, Secretary Bessent has been more focused on the 10-year Treasury yield coming down, as that has more impact on mortgage rates, car loans and business lending.

The Treasury has made a big bet on this occurring, as it has been funding the government’s borrowing needs with Treasury Bills, because Bessent feels long rates are too high to term out the government’s borrowing, thereby locking in a high borrowing cost.

This hasn’t happened yet, though, as while the Fed Funds Rate has declined by 175 basis points since the Fed began easing, the 10-year Treasury yield has actually risen by 63 basis points.

By conducting a rate check on the yen, the Treasury is helping the BOJ support their currency during a period of internal turmoil in Japan. But it is also moving to protect the Treasury market from possible sales of Treasury securities by Japan, the largest foreign holder of U.S. government debt. Another impact, of course, of weakening the dollar in support of the yen, is to improve our trade exports.

data.title: Cipher Mining: Execution Replaces Speculation In 2026
data.description:
Cipher Mining (CIFR) has been one of the Bitcoin miners executing well these past years. I've been long CIFR for the past few years. My past coverage of the company reflects this stance, which all carried Buy ratings. In between the coverages, there have been good runs and sometimes retracements. In my last coverage I highlighted the point that Cipher Mining’s rerating story isn't over yet.

CIFR is up a lot from 2024, but currently off 2025 highs. In this piece, I'd look at what lies ahead for 2026 as a Bitcoin miner and a HPC/AI play, and also touch on the HPC deals secured last year and the implication of those deals into the present thesis.

Cipher Mining - From Pipeline to Paychecks in 2026

Cipher Mining is one of the HPC/AI plays expecting an operational inflection point in 2026. The market at the moment has remained muted since the HPC/AI hype late last year for the miners that secured deals.

​They all rerated in Q4, but have now since mostly retraced those gains. CIFR itself surged to a 52-week high of ~$25 on the news of the $3 billion 10-year Fluidstack lease deal last September, then followed by the $5.5 billion AWS deal, before retracing following the issuance of senior secured notes totalling $1.73 billion in mid November.

I'd like to say that the market seems exhausted in assigning premium to miners turned HPC plays based on just announced deals, pipeline capacity, and projected cash flow discounted into today's share price. I highlighted these in an article published here on Seeking Alpha last Friday covering Hut 8 Corp. (HUT), another miner turned HPC play. While each of these miners have secured what looks like similar multi-billion HPC deals, nuances in the deals themselves, as well as each miner's equity composition (of which I'm currently weighing their specific exposure to Bitcoin volatility through their balance sheets), and projected net operating income [NOI] for the duration of the deal are the specific metrics I'm looking at as I analyze these companies.

​In my latest HUT article, I downgraded HUT from a Buy to a Hold, despite HUT having seen similar capacity development and deals win as the rest of the HPC pivot companies. The main reason was the longer-dated timeline for their River Bend site energization compared to peers, which was compounded by the company's exposure to Bitcoin as it holds over 13,000 BTC on the balance sheet. These present a double headwind for the company, making 2026 a potentially muted year for HUT.

​In contrast, as I analyze Cipher Mining going into 2026, I'd be maintaining a Buy for CIFR for the singular reason that the checks for the HPC lease from AWS are projected to hit the books from August this year, and 168 MW Fluidstack hosting capacity at Barber Lake expects to commence rent in September. Impressive top line numbers making the headline are a first place to watch for the next catalysts.

​I’ve talked about the sentiment of the current market, which has apparently moved beyond maintaining momentum based on just pipeline capacity or capacity under exclusivity. Those were last year's catalysts. 2026 is the execution year, when HPC pivots show actual top-line impact on financials.

To project what's coming for Cipher, I'd make some assumptions. Take the Cipher's base mining revenue to be ~$55 million per quarter (I derived that using the average revenue of three quarters already reported in FY25); annualized, this gives a steady mining run rate of ~$220 million. I'm quite positive the company can now produce that amount of quarterly revenue from mining as a steady run rate till the next halving, with current hashrate at 23.5 EH/s and mining efficiency looking good at 16.8 J/Th.

Now layer in the HPC contracts. Cipher has roughly $8.5 billion of contracted revenue from the AWS and Fluidstack deals. If this revenue is recognized on a straight-line basis over a 13-year weighted contract life, it implies ~$650 million in annual HPC revenue (calculated as $8.5 billion for the combined deal divided by 13 years). And would put total sales from both mining and HPC to about $870 million. Note that my estimate is still a conservative one as I use a straight line assumption for the modeling.

Cipher's adjusted EBITDA margin improved greatly in 2025. It was just around 12% in Q1 2025 on $49 million revenue and $6.1 million adjusted earnings, but jumped to ~60% in Q3 on a $72.0 million revenue and ~$41 million adjusted EBITDA in Q3. I believe higher adjusted EBITDA is sustainable because it was driven by the upgrade of the mining fleets to from older Bitmain T21s to S21 xp Antminers. Economies of scale are likely to stay intact in the meantime with factors like network difficulty and Bitcoin price not moving too much.

For the purpose of my modeling for projected earnings, I'd however factor in volatility and go with a conservative ~50% adjusted EBITDA for the mining operations moving forward, which I believe the company can sustain considering the improved 16.8 J/Th average efficiency the S21 xp Antminers has brought to the mining fleet.

Valuation is bound to shift from Cipher's current life as a Bitcoin miner and when the HPC checks hit in H2. In the deals, management is guiding for 80 - 85% NOI margin, which I believe is very well within range, as the AWS deal is a triple N, meaning the clients take care of the biggest three costs (power, cooling, and facility costs). For this modelling, I'll be treating NOI margin as EBITDA margin which is safe enough considering low overhead and other operational levers in the HPC lease deal, which when accounted for won't skew our EBITDA from NOI estimate materially.

Segment

Annual Revenue Projection ~

Adj. EBITDA Margin

Annual EBITDA Contribution

Bitcoin Mining

$870 Million
