$662.5 Million

At $6.94 billion market cap at present and $870M projected 2027 sales, CIFR trades at ~7.9x sales, meaning at current market cap CIFR is trading around 7.9x projected sales. A ~7.9x forward sales multiple for a company with 75%+ blended EBITDA margins and contracted revenue from AWS and Google can be considered modest. Traditional data center providers like Equinix (EQIX) and Digital Reality (DLR) typically trade in the low mid teens to sales, but they have much slower growth rates as they are well established firms beyond the rapid growth phase. The case for CIFR is that it is combining high-margin HPC revenue with optionality in Bitcoin upside and potential for rapid growth, which are visible through the pipeline capacity and the AWS and Fluidstack deals themselves which have clauses that allow expansion down the line.

Using the current $6.78 billion EV and our blended EBITDA projection of $662.5 million from both segments, implies an EV/EBITDA [fwd] of 10.2x (gotten from EV divided by projected EBITDA), and the valuation gets even more attractive.

At 10.2x 2027 EBITDA, CIFR is being priced as if the HPC deals are still high-risk speculations. The market is ignoring the fact that the AWS and Fluidstack contracts are legally binding and the sites already in build-out, the market is underpricing the near certainty of the coming cash flows.

As the HPC revenues start to come in, if CIFR catches up to the Sales multiple of data center provider peers (moving from 8x to 10 - 12x), that would imply a ~25% upside for the rerating from current stock price. If CIFR catches up to the EBITDA multiple (moving from 10.2x to 20 - 30x), the stock would more than double.

Risks

The risk I worry about most at this stage is construction and energization slippage risk. As I have noted with Hut 8 and why I downgraded to a Hold because of longer-dated energization timelines, while peers are already locked in for HPC revenue just months away. The market punishes longer-dated timelines. Any hint that the August 2026 for AWS site launch and October for Fluidstack site launch is sliding into 2027 might trigger a sharp CIFR sell-off.

Takeaway

The market moved with momentum on the AWS deal news in November and is now under-appreciating it in January. The gap between current prices around $17 and the top last November around $25 is the execution premium that will likely return as we get closer to that first lease payment in 2H this year.

We can do as much rough math as we want, but unless the HPC lease payments don’t start hitting the top line in September/October, CIFR will likely trade as a momentum stock in the latter part of this year, to be driven by the headline top-line numbers from the HPC deals.

data.title: VIG: Dividend Investors Should Be Careful (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
Vanguard Dividend Appreciation Index Fund ETF Shares (VIG) has started 2026 with a gain of 1.5%, which is ahead of the S&P 500 increase of 1.1%. Since I initiated coverage almost three months ago, explaining why I don't dig VIG, it has gained 2.7%, while SPDR S&P 500 Trust ETF (SPY) has gained 0.6%. It's all good, right? No, as over the past year, returning 12.1% it has still performed worse than SPY and also Vanguard S&P 500 (VOO) , which have both returned 14.4%.

VIG tracks the S&P U.S. Dividend Growers Index, which will be rebalanced in March. The index does track large-cap stocks, so comparing the return to SPY or VOO does make sense. At year-end, the index had its top 10 names representing 35.1% of it. Standard & Poor's shares the breakdown by sector as of year-end:

It also makes sense to compare VIG to other dividend-focused ETFs. I include seven others on my ETF Watch List that totals 80 ETFs. I reiterated my bullishness on recently,and it is one of them. VIG has been the worst performer relative to these peers in January so far and the second-worst over the past three months. The worst has been Invesco High Yield Equity Dividend Achievers ETF (PEY).

In this follow-up to my initial review of VIG, I compare it to the peers and conclude that at this higher price, my rating should move from Sell to Strong Sell.

How VIG Has Performed

Here is the year-to-date action for VIG and seven other dividend-focused ETFs that I follow closely:

While it is up the least, it has outpaced the group substantially since the end of 2022:

As much as it is up relative to peers, it has lagged VOO, which has returned 88.0% since the end of 2022. I discuss the two at the bottom below as potential alternatives.

Looking at the VIG chart, it set an all-time high recently:

Since the low in April it has rallied 31.8% after the recent pullback. I see support at $215 and then $204. At the low in 2020, it traded below $90. A 38.2% retracement from the recent peak to that level would result in a drop to about $174, which is just above the April low and would be a decline of 22% from the current price.

The Challenges VIG May Face

When I initiated coverage in late October, I suggested that its large exposure to Technology had helped it do well. While 27% is lower than the S&P 500 exposure of 34.4%, it is quite high. If Technology, a big winner over the past three years pulls back, it could hurt VIG. More specifically, if the large Technology stocks that VIG holds go down, it could hurt the ETF. Among the top 10 names in the ETF at year-end were Broadcom (AVGO), Microsoft (MSFT) and Apple (AAPL), totaling 15.2% as the top three holdings. All three are down by more than 3.6% in 2026.

The low exposure to Utilities may hurt VIG too. I initiated State Street Utilities Select Sector SPDR ETF (XLU) with a Sell, but moved it to Hold recently after it declined. My issue with the large-cap Utilities has been the market-cap weighted exposure to just a few of them. The good news is that VIG doesn't own the ones that concerned me. The challenge, though, and why I upgraded XLU to Hold from Sell, is that interest-rates could fall, which would perhaps shift demand away from more consumer-oriented sectors. The index that VIG is tracking is quite low in Utilities.

While Financials have gone up since I warned about State Street® Financial Select Sector SPDR® ETF (XLF) in early June, they have certainly lagged. The risks that I pointed out for XLF remain, and VIG has a lot of exposure to large-cap Financials. Three of the top ten names are big Financials companies, and they are all down in 2026 by 7% or more. This is the second-largest part of VIG and is substantially higher than the S&P 500 exposure.

So, weakness in its two very large sectors could hurt VIG. Of course, we could see a rebound in these stocks, which have declined so far. The ETF has a very large-cap focus. Vanguard on the VIG page of its website shares that at year-end the median market cap of the ETF was $277 billion. This is smaller than VOO at $382 billion, but it is quite big. The year has started with smaller companies doing better than larger-ones, and it could keep happening. Over the past three years, the S&P 500 has returned almost three times the 31.6% return of the Russell 2000. If large-caps continue to underperform, it could weigh upon VIG.

Alternatives for Investors

Here is an update of the table I have shared for the dividend-focused ETFs:

VIG has an extremely low management fee and it is the largest of these dividend-focused ETFs in assets. Its return over the past year is near the high of the group, and its five-year return has been the best among them. It also has the lowest dividend yield, though it is a premium to the dividend yields of VOO and SPY.

When I wrote about it in December, I said that PEY was okay, and I rated it a Hold despite my bearishness. It has rallied 4.1% in total return compared to a slight decline in the S&P 500 since then. I pointed out that its management fee of 0.54% is much higher than VIG, but I think that it is not so high that investors should not consider it. The ETF is much smaller than VIG and holds just 50 stocks measured against a different index. After I wrote that piece, my wife added some to her IRA, and I included a 10% position in an ETF model portfolio that I created and shared at year-end.

While it is up a lot so far in 2026, I continue to like ProShares Russell 2000 Dividend Growers ETF (SMDV), as I explained earlier this month. It is focused on high-quality small-caps and uses equal weighting rather than market-cap weighting. PEY is focused on large-caps, but SMDV small-cap focus may keep some investors away from it. My wife owns this one in her IRA too, and it is currently 15.4% of my ETF model portfolio that holds seven ETFs.

For investors looking at dividend ETFs, I do like these two that I have discussed here, though there may be some others that could make more sense. I view the dividend-yield of a stock as just one thing and would never prioritize that yield. I am not saying that investors should sell VIG because it is the lowest dividend yield of these eight. While I like PEY, it is not because of its higher yield. The one I like the most of these, SMDV, has a below-average dividend yield.

I currently have a 15% position in Invesco S&P 500 Equal Weight ETF (RSP) in that model portfolio and initiated it with a Hold rating at the end of November, calling it a unique and compelling ETF. It has outpaced SPY and VOO since then, and has returned 3.8% so far in 2026. RSP has a lot fewer Seeking Alpha followers at 21K. For those who care about the dividend yield, RSP is slightly higher than VIG.

Conclusion

12 weeks after I initiated VIG with a Sell rating, it has advanced slightly. I am downgrading VIG to Strong Sell. If readers own it, they can sell at a slightly higher price. While it has lagged some of the ETFs that make more sense, I am suggesting that these alternatives make sense.

data.title: Expedia: Strong Results, Improving US Demand, Raised Guidance, And Attractive Valuation
data.description:
We are returning to Expedia (EXPE) after roughly a year (Fig. 1). While we have not provided quarterly updates throughout the period, the company and its industry dynamics have remained under close observation, and we have recently published several related analyses, including: Trip.com (Antitrust Overhang Creates An Attractive Entry Point) and Tripadvisor (Strategic Pivot Underway, Compelling Upside Ahead).

Our buy case rested on 4 pillars:

  1. Expedia’s clear valuation gap versus peers (both on P/E and EV/EBITDA),
  2. evidence of better top-line traction helped by easier year-on-year comparisons,
  3. leadership refresh with a new CFO (this was also marked by Wall Street sentiment improvement towards the new management), and
  4. a balance sheet that remains lightly levered alongside continued share repurchases.

One year later, we report a total return of almost 58%. After a thorough sector review, also informed by McKinsey (The Evolving Role of Experiences in Travel), we still see upside ahead.

Why are we still positive?

Here at the Lab, the most meaningful sector takeaway came from OpenAI's latest change to ChatGPT's advertising. Over the last few months, we have come to understand that bears are countering the change in client interaction driven by AI disruption. With OpenAI starting to test ads in ChatGPT, we believe this implies that AI will no longer be disruptive, but will work more like Google. Thus, online travel platforms might potentially benefit from it. So, we currently view Expedia as a one-stop shop with supportive inventory fragmentation. By leveraging our cross-coverage on Trip.com and McKinsey report , the travel experience market could exceed $1 trillion, and Expedia adapted its search tool with Packages and Things to Do. With Expedia's data, the company will likely target younger demographics, taking advantage of consumer preferences for experiences and tourism at the expense of traditional goods ownership.

Fig 1

Looking at the Q3 results, Expedia room nights accelerated to +11% year over year (Fig. 2). This was backed by broad-based strength across regions. More importantly, Expedia reported better numbers than its peers over the same period. In numbers, Airbnb was up by +9% and Booking by +8%. As shown in Fig. 2, the B2B business remains the primary driver of consolidated growth, lifting the group’s overall trajectory by +26% year over year. In comparison, the B2C franchise also re-accelerated to +7% yearly, easing concerns that this segment is structurally rolling over. Additionally, and also aligned with inventory supply, the more important point is Expedia's progress in tightening its value proposition. Expedia is better aligning its product offering and merchandising across its core markets.

Source: Expedia Q3 2025 ppt - Fig 2

Given the positive results, during the analyst call, Expedia management confirmed that the US market is improving and Expedia is executing well into that tailwind. For this reason, the group raised the Fiscal Year 2025 guidance at +7% Gross Bookings and +6–7% top-line sales. This is above the initial +4–6% range communicated on the Q4 2024 call. Here at the Lab, aside from the guidance raise, we continue to favor Expedia given its durable formula (EPS growth > EBITDA growth> revenue growth - Fig. 3).

Fig 3

Someone could argue that B2C bookings' growth of 7% continues to lag behind B2B. Others are questioning the "moderated pace" margin expansion in Fiscal Year 2026. That said, we view this as overly conservative. From a bull-case perspective, Q3 checks the boxes, and Expedia continues to trade conservatively versus its direct peers. Room Nights reached 108 million, and there is potential for various growth initiatives in 2026. The peers' check below is supported by our recent assessment of Starboard Value’s presentation (Fig. 4). More importantly, Expedia's growth trajectory is higher than its peers.

Fig 4

Earnings Changes and Valuation

Even accounting for a 56.55% increase in stock price, we believe Expedia has upside potential. The company is consolidating its loyalty programs and tech stacks. This will improve retention and cross-selling and should drive benefits to its core operating profit margin. In our numbers, rolling forward our estimate to 2026, we project a 10% sales growth to $16.2 billion. Considering the higher 2025 guidance and this impressive B2B segment growth, we are confident Expedia will likely achieve an EBIT margin of at least 21%. Given a D&A of $750/$800 million, Expedia's 2026 EBITDA is forecasted at $4.15 billion. Given its current net debt position, Expedia has almost no financial obligations (Fig. 5), even after accounting for cash and short-term investments. So, with a current market cap of around 8.3x, which is not far from Starboard Value's valuation. Continuing to value Expedia at 11.0x EBITDA (unchanged from our previous valuation and in line with the company's historical average), we arrive at an equity value of $45.65 billion. This represents an upside of 32%. Our new target price is set at $328.5 per share.

Fig 5

Risks

Expedia’s downside cases include FX volatility, failure to leverage the fixed cost base, and wage inflation. The company might be impacted by higher marketing costs and less efficient marketing spend. Additionally, as reported in the analysis, AI remains a question mark for the travel experience, even though we are positive about it. The company is exposed to competition from major OTA travel companies such as Booking and Airbnb, as well as softer consumer demand. Google also might push further into online travel.

Conclusion

In a nutshell, we believe Expedia's shares still have upside in capital appreciation. We remain constructive on the travel sector, and Expedia remains inexpensive relative to its larger peers and has many opportunities to continue achieving a positive, profitable growth trajectory at double-digit rates.

data.title: ConocoPhillips Has Future Appreciation Potential
data.description:
ConocoPhillips (COP) is a major upstream-only oil company with a $120 billion market cap. The company is committed to future growth with the production of millions of barrels/day and a number of exciting capital projects, yet its share price has been relatively flat since we recommended investing last.

As we'll see throughout this article, the company is committed to future shareholder returns and growth, making it a valuable investment.

ConocoPhillips Quarterly Highlights

ConocoPhillips had a strong quarter with $2.5 billion in FCF for the quarter, with $5.4 billion in CFO and continued capex.

That puts the company at an 8% FCF yield, a reasonable FCF yield given the growth potential that the company has. The company's strong production has enabled it to come in at the high end of its guidance, pushing 2.4 million barrels/day in production. The company has continued to improve its asset base, and it's increased its dividend by 8%.

That means its yield at the time is ~3.2%. Strategically, ConocoPhillips has a number of expansion projects through Willow and PALNG. These projects will cost billions in capital but are exciting new opportunities. The company's outlook is for continued cost management with a focus on strong operations and incremental FCF going into 2029.

ConocoPhillips Growth Investments

ConocoPhillips is continuing to invest heavily in growth, despite rising costs.

The Willow Project is expected to cost $8.5-9 billion, of which more than half has been spent. The company spent $2 billion in 2025, and for 2026 to 2028, it's expected to be $1.7 billion/year before the project finishes up and comes online in 2029. However, with strong FCF, the project should still be able to justify itself as an investment.

For the company's LNG project, total capital has been reduced to $3.4 billion, with a $600 million PALNG credit. 80% of the LNG project is capital complete with only $0.8 billion of spending remaining. The company expects the first LNG for NFE in 2026. For reference, ConocoPhillips only has a 3.125% stake in the massive project.

ConocoPhillips is focused on a purpose-built LNG strategy with global scale. With NFE/NFS, the company's resource LNG is expected to grow to 10 MTPA, with both resource and commercial LNG growing in kind to 2032. North American gas is valuable, low-cost gas that can go through other markets, with ConocoPhillips controlling a valuable supply chain.

ConocoPhillips 2026

Looking at ConocoPhillips for 2026, the company has substantial potential to drive shareholder returns.

ConocoPhillips is continuing to manage its capital spending with a substantial portion of growth projects already paid. 2026 capital guidance is $3 billion/quarter, down by $400 million/quarter from 2024. At the same time, the company is managing its core costs, with $10.2 billion in 2026 guidance down $1 billion from 2024.

Despite all of this, ConocoPhillips is guiding for 0-2% production growth. It outperformed the midpoint of guidance in 2025, and it'll expect modest growth in 2026 with manageable capital and costs.

Going from 2026 to 2028, ConocoPhillips is expecting ~$1 billion per annum of improvement in FCF. The company has new LNG startups and cost/margin enhancements along with a massive jump in 2029 as the 180 thousand barrels/day Willow project starts up with a 30-year reserve life, boosting the company substantially.

At current WTI prices, that means ~$6 billion in incremental FCF. That puts the company at $16 billion in 2029 FCF, a comfortable 13% FCF yield in just a few years from those who invest today. That will enable the company to repurchase shares and increase dividends, generating strong shareholder returns.

ConocoPhillips Shareholder Returns

ConocoPhillips ability to generate shareholder returns is clearly visible.

ConocoPhillips has earned $6.1 billion in YTD FCF along with $2 billion from working capital and dispositions. That's because the company has had a substantial $9.5 billion in capital expenditures. After that, the company has spent $7.9 billion on shareholder returns ($10.5 billion annualized). That's a high single-digit FCF yield, one that the company can comfortably afford.

The company finished with slightly more cash than it started with. This shows the company's commitment to valuable long-term returns.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is crude oil prices. As seen above, the company's $7 billion in incremental FCF improvement drops to $6 billion with WTI dropping to $60/barrel. With the potential for weaker oil prices and ongoing trade wars and economic issues, that could substantially hurt ConocoPhillips ability to drive future shareholder returns.

Conclusion

ConocoPhillips is a strong, pure-play upstream company, with a market cap of just over $120 billion. The company has continued to generate strong cash flow, focusing on share buybacks and dividends, with a high single-digit dividend yield. The company has done that while being in a phase of investing massively in its business.

However, the company is going through an inflection point. FCF is expected to expand by roughly $1 billion/year for the next several years before spiking by a massive $4 billion in 2029, supported by the Willow Project startup. That combination of factors makes ConocoPhillips today a great investment. Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.
